text: The monomer units of proteins are:
explanation: Proteins: polymers of О±-amino acids linked by peptide bonds (вЂ“COвЂ“NHвЂ“). 20 standard amino acids form all proteins.
name: Protein Monomers

text: The peptide bond in proteins is formed between:
options: вЂ“OH of one amino acid and вЂ“OH of another
вЂ“COOH of one amino acid and вЂ“NHв‚‚ of another (with loss of Hв‚‚O)
explanation: Peptide bond: вЂ“COвЂ“NHвЂ“, formed by condensation of вЂ“COOH + вЂ“NHв‚‚ в†’ вЂ“COвЂ“NHвЂ“ + Hв‚‚O. Amide linkage.
name: Peptide Bond Formation

text: The О±-helix and ОІ-pleated sheet are examples of:
explanation: Secondary structure: regular folding maintained by H-bonds between C=O and NвЂ“H of peptide bonds. О±-helix (coiled) and ОІ-sheet (pleated).
name: Protein Secondary Structure

text: The molecular formula of glucose is:
explanation: Glucose (Cв‚†Hв‚Ѓв‚‚Oв‚†): aldohexose, monosaccharide. Sucrose (Cв‚Ѓв‚‚Hв‚‚в‚‚Oв‚Ѓв‚Ѓ): disaccharide. Starch/cellulose: (Cв‚†Hв‚Ѓв‚ЂOв‚…)в‚™.
name: Molecular Formula of Glucose

explanation: Sucrose + Hв‚‚O (acid/enzyme) в†’ glucose + fructose. Called 'invert sugar' because optical rotation changes from + to вЂ“ on hydrolysis.
name: Hydrolysis of Sucrose

text: The linkage between monosaccharides in starch is:
options: О±-1,4-glycosidic bond (amylose) and О±-1,6 at branch points (amylopectin)
explanation: Starch: amylose has О±-1,4-glycosidic bonds (unbranched). Amylopectin has О±-1,4 bonds + О±-1,6 at branch points. Cellulose: ОІ-1,4 bonds.
name: Glycosidic Bond in Starch

text: Enzymes are biological catalysts that are:
explanation: Enzymes are mostly globular proteins. Exception: ribozymes are catalytic RNA molecules. Highly specific, work at body temperature and neutral pH.
name: Nature of Enzymes

text: The lock and key model of enzyme action states:
options: Enzyme changes shape to fit substrate
Active site of enzyme has specific shape complementary to substrate
Any substrate can bind to any enzyme
explanation: Lock-and-key model (Fischer): rigid active site is exactly complementary to the substrate (specific key fits specific lock). No conformational change.
name: Lock and Key Model

text: DNA and RNA differ in that DNA has:
options: Deoxyribose sugar and thymine (instead of uracil)
explanation: DNA: deoxyribose + bases A, T, G, C (thymine). RNA: ribose + A, U (uracil), G, C. The 2'вЂ“OH group is absent in DNA.
name: DNA vs RNA

explanation: Fat-soluble: A, D, E, K (stored in body fat and liver). Water-soluble: B-complex and C (must be taken regularly). Excess fat-soluble в†’ toxicity possible.
name: Fat-Soluble Vitamins

text: Soaps are sodium or potassium salts of:
explanation: Soap: sodium/potassium salts of long-chain fatty acids (Cв‚Ѓв‚‚вЂ“Cв‚Ѓв‚€). Made by saponification: fat + NaOH в†’ soap + glycerol.
name: Soap Formation

text: The cleansing action of soap works because soap molecules have:
options: A hydrophilic (ionic) head and hydrophobic (alkyl) tail вЂ” forms micelles
explanation: Soap: long hydrocarbon tail (hydrophobic, dissolves in oil) + carboxylate head (hydrophilic, dissolves in water). Micelles trap dirt in centre.
name: Cleansing Action of Soap

explanation: Analgesics: pain-relieving drugs. Non-narcotic (aspirin, paracetamol) and narcotic (morphine, codeine). Aspirin also antipyretic and anti-inflammatory.
name: Analgesics

text: Antibiotics differ from antiseptics in that antibiotics:
options: Are taken internally to kill/inhibit bacteria without harming host cells
explanation: Antibiotics: taken internally (systemic), selective toxicity (harm bacteria, not host). Antiseptics: applied to living tissue externally. Disinfectants: non-living surfaces.
name: Antibiotics vs Antiseptics

text: Detergents (synthetic) differ from soaps in that detergents:
options: Are not effective in hard water
Work in hard water and acidic solutions (no scum formation)
explanation: Synthetic detergents: sulfonate/sulfate groups в†’ calcium/magnesium salts are soluble в†’ no scum in hard water. Soaps form insoluble scum with CaВІвЃє/MgВІвЃє.
name: Detergents vs Soaps
